story.headline: US strikes reached closer to Tehran than at any point in this war overnight, as Iran claimed retaliatory hits on US bases across the Gulf
story.summary: CENTCOM struck sites near Tehran and disabled an oil tanker enforcing a naval blockade, while Iran claimed retaliatory strikes on US assets in Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan and Iraq; 35+ dead in the latest wave.
story.display_body: US Central Command said it struck command centres, air-defence sites and missile and drone facilities at Bandar Abbas and, in a 90-minute wave late Wednesday, coastal-defence and cruise-missile positions on Greater Tunb Island near the Strait of Hormuz; it also said it disabled a non-compliant oil tanker with Hellfire missiles to enforce a naval blockade reimposed on Tuesday 14 July. Iranian media reported strikes across at least nine locations and a hospital in Ahvaz evacuating 211 patients; Iran's army in turn claimed attacks on US radar and Patriot systems at Ali Al Salem airbase in Kuwait, the Sheikh Isa base in Bahrain and al-Azraq in Jordan, while Jordan said it intercepted eight Iranian missiles. At least 35 people have died and more than 300 been wounded since this wave began around Wednesday 8 July, and the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps threatened to halt all Middle East energy exports over the blockade: either for everyone or for no one.
story.why: A direct US-Iran exchange now straddling the Strait of Hormuz - the chokepoint for roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil - with an explicit threat to the region's energy exports is the single most consequential escalation risk on the board, and the first strikes near Tehran mark a widening, not a winding-down.

story.headline: Ukraine's parliament confirms Naftogaz chief Serhii Koretskyi as prime minister
story.summary: Ukraine's parliament approved Serhii Koretskyi as PM with 289 votes in a Zelenskyy wartime reshuffle, amid protests over the dismissal of Defence Minister Fedorov.
story.display_body: Koretskyi, who ran the state energy company Naftogaz, was approved with 289 votes; Zelenskyy argued his energy record made him best placed to steer Ukraine through another wartime winter of attacks on the power grid. The vote came as hundreds protested outside parliament against the dismissal of Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov after only six months, with critics warning it could disrupt military reforms mid-war. Overnight, Russia again hit Kyiv with ballistic missiles, killing at least two, according to Ukrainian authorities, while Ukrainian drones struck a residential building in the southern Russian city of Engels on the Volga.
story.why: A government shake-up at the top of a country under daily bombardment is a real governance risk - the reshuffle trades continuity for Zelenskyy's bet that an energy technocrat can keep the lights on through winter.

story.headline: More than 500 Rohingya feared dead after two boats capsize off Myanmar
story.summary: The IOM and UNHCR said two boats carrying mostly Rohingya that left Rakhine State in late June capsized, with more than 500 feared dead.
story.display_body: The IOM (International Organization for Migration) and UNHCR (the UN refugee agency) said the two vessels, carrying mostly Rohingya, left Myanmar's Rakhine State in late June before sinking; survivor accounts underpin the toll. The stateless, mostly Muslim Rohingya - some 1.2 million of whom remain in overcrowded camps in Bangladesh after fleeing a 2017 military campaign several countries have called genocide - increasingly attempt the perilous sea crossing to Malaysia, a route on which nearly 900 died or went missing in 2025, its deadliest year on record.
story.why: If confirmed, this would be one of the worst single losses of life on what the UNHCR calls the world's deadliest maritime migration route, and the agencies tie it directly to aid cuts that have slashed rations in the Bangladesh camps.

fields.display_body: The IOM (International Organization for Migration) and UNHCR (the UN refugee agency) said the two vessels, carrying mostly Rohingya, left Myanmar's Rakhine State in late June before sinking; survivor accounts underpin the toll. story.headline: European rights court condemns Switzerland over vegan meals for detainees
story.summary: The ECtHR found Switzerland violated Article 9 freedom of conscience by denying vegan meals to a detainee in Geneva and an interned patient in Vaud (Cery).
story.display_body: The court in Strasbourg found that refusing an animal-rights activist held on remand at Geneva's Champ-Dollon prison, and a patient in the Cery unit, meals consistent with their vegan convictions - and giving them no way to challenge that before Swiss courts - violated their freedom of conscience under Article 9 of the European Convention (freedom of thought, conscience and religion). The activist had been arrested in 2018 over a series of break-ins at slaughterhouses and butchers' shops in western Switzerland and held about 11 months.
story.why: The ruling pushes ethical veganism toward the same Article 9 protection as religious diets and obliges Swiss detention and psychiatric facilities - cantonal responsibilities, directly touching Vaud and Geneva - to accommodate it or face Strasbourg.

fields.display_body: The court in Strasbourg found that refusing an animal-rights activist held on remand at Geneva's Champ-Dollon prison, and a patient in the Cery unit, meals consistent with their vegan convictions - and giving them no way to challenge that before Swiss courts - violated their freedom of conscience under Article 9 of the European Convention (freedom of thought, conscience and religion). story.headline: Vaud and Valais lift their heatwave plans as the canicule eases
story.summary: Vaud and Valais stood down their formal heatwave plans as the hot spell eased while urging continued health vigilance.
story.display_body: The two western cantons stood down the emergency measures triggered during the recent heatwave - coordination of care homes, hospitals and public-health messaging - but stressed that vulnerable people remain at risk and that the sanitary alert is being relaxed, not cancelled.
story.why: A concrete, local marker that the heat episode straining Vaud's health services has broken, though the caution flags how these plans have become a recurring summer fixture.

fields.display_body: The two western cantons stood down the emergency measures triggered during the recent heatwave - coordination of care homes, hospitals and public-health messaging - but stressed that vulnerable people remain at risk and that the sanitary alert is being relaxed, not cancelled.

story.headline: French-Swiss arms-trafficking network dismantled, 12 arrested
story.summary: Twelve people including a police officer were arrested over a French-Swiss weapons-trafficking ring that sourced arms in Switzerland to sell across France, Lille prosecutors said.
story.display_body: A raid on 23 June across three French departements and Switzerland, coordinated with Swiss authorities and Europol, led to 10 arrests in France and two in Switzerland; nine were charged on 26 June with illegal possession, manufacture or trafficking of war materiel, weapons and munitions as part of an organised criminal group. The gang is alleged to have stocked up on weapons in Switzerland and resold them across France.
story.why: It puts Switzerland squarely in the supply chain for firearms feeding French organised crime, and the arrest of a police officer sharpens the case.

story.headline: Seven drones sighted near the Gösgen nuclear plant
story.summary: A resident reported at least seven drones flying toward the Gösgen nuclear plant in Solothurn on Sunday 12 July evening, prompting a police operation of unclear cause.
story.display_body: A resident of Niedergosgen alerted police around 22:30 after spotting the drones heading for the plant; Solothurn cantonal police confirmed the response and are checking flight paths in coordination with the federal civil-aviation office (BAZL) and federal police (Fedpol). The plant's information chief said security was never compromised - the reactor is built to withstand even a large aircraft crash - and that the drone threat to nuclear safety is currently deemed negligible, though a similar 2025 sighting over the Laufenburg power hub turned out to be an authorised Swissgrid inspection flight.
story.why: It adds a nuclear site to a run of recent drone sightings over Swiss military and energy infrastructure (Jassbach and Meiringen this month), keeping critical-infrastructure surveillance on the political agenda even when the drones prove benign.
